section.title: The LORD's Lawsuit and What He Requires
section.sectionRationale: After the summit of the Bethlehem Ruler, Micah returns to the courtroom. The LORD summons the mountains as witnesses and brings a covenant lawsuit against His people: what have I done to weary you? He rehearses His saving acts, exposes the emptiness of religion offered without a changed heart, and gives the famous summary of true religion in 6:8. The chapter then reads the verdict over a city full of dishonest scales and violence.
section.thesis: The God who redeemed His people has a righteous case against their ingratitude and injustice; He does not want mountains of sacrifice but justice, mercy, and a humble walk with Himself, and where these are refused He justly sentences a corrupt society to futility.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the covenant lawsuit in which God recalls His redemption and asks what He has done wrong (1-5); what the LORD actually requires, over against costly ritual, namely to do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly (6-8); and the verdict against a city of dishonest gain and violence (9-16).

units.label: The covenant lawsuit
units.note: plead your case before the mountains

verses.text: Hear now what the LORD says: “Arise, plead your case before the mountains, and let the hills hear your voice.
Hear, O mountains, the LORD’s {{rib|indictment}}, you enduring foundations of the earth. For the LORD has a case against His people, and He will argue it against Israel:
‘My people, what have I done to you? Testify against Me how I have wearied you!
For I brought you up from the land of Egypt and redeemed you from the house of slavery. I sent Moses before you, as well as Aaron and Miriam.
My people, remember what Balak king of Moab counseled and what Balaam son of Beor answered. Remember your journey from Shittim to Gilgal, so that you may acknowledge the righteousness of the LORD.’”

units.label: What the LORD requires
units.note: do justice, love mercy, walk humbly

verses.text: With what shall I come before the LORD when I bow before the God on high? Should I come to Him with burnt offerings, with year-old calves?
Would the LORD be pleased with thousands of rams, with ten thousand rivers of oil? Shall I present my firstborn for my transgression, the fruit of my body for the sin of my soul?
He has shown you, O man, what is good. And what does the LORD require of you but to act {{mishpat|justly}}, to love {{chesed|mercy}}, and to {{tsana|walk humbly}} with your God?

units.label: The verdict on a corrupt city

verses.text: The voice of the LORD calls out to the city (and it is sound wisdom to fear Your name): “Heed the rod and the One who ordained it.
Can I forget any longer, O house of the wicked, the treasures of wickedness and the short ephah, which is accursed?
Can I excuse dishonest scales or bags of false weights?
For the wealthy of the city are full of violence, and its residents speak lies; their tongues are deceitful in their mouths.
Therefore I am striking you severely, to ruin you because of your sins.
You will eat but not be satisfied, and your hunger will remain with you. What you acquire, you will not preserve; and what you save, I will give to the sword.
You will sow but not reap; you will press olives but not anoint yourselves with oil; you will tread grapes but not drink the wine.

You have kept the statutes of Omri and all the practices of Ahab’s house; you have followed their counsel. Therefore I will make you a desolation, and your inhabitants an object of contempt; you will bear the scorn of the nations.”

rib.gloss: A legal dispute, controversy, or lawsuit. The prophets picture the covenant relationship as a court case: the LORD, the wronged party, summons creation itself as witness and brings formal charges against His people for breaking the covenant. It is a lawsuit born of love, meant not merely to condemn but to bring the guilty to acknowledge the truth and return.

mishpat.gloss: Justice, right judgment, the fair ordering of life that gives each person their due. To do mishpat is not a feeling but action: treating others rightly, defending the weak, refusing dishonest gain. God requires that His redeemed people mirror His own just character in how they live with one another.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast love, covenant loyalty, mercy that keeps faith. It is the very kindness God shows His people, and He asks them to love it, not merely perform it. To love chesed is to delight in showing loyal, merciful kindness to others because one has received it from God; the same word crowns the book at 7:18 and 7:20.

tsana.gloss: To be humble, modest, lowly. The phrase to walk humbly with your God describes a whole life lived in modest, dependent step with the LORD, neither proud before Him nor self-reliant. Justice and mercy toward others flow from this lowly, trusting walk with God Himself.

basic.context: Micah imagines a courtroom. God calls the mountains and hills as witnesses and puts His own people on trial, yet He begins not by listing their sins but by asking, my people, what have I done to you? He reminds them how He rescued them from Egypt and carried them through the wilderness. Then comes the heart of the chapter: the people ask what offering could satisfy God, piling up calves, rams, rivers of oil, even their own children, and Micah answers that God has already shown them what is good, to do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly with Him. The chapter ends with the sentence on a city that cheats with false weights and is full of violence.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a covenant lawsuit, a form the prophets use to confront a people who have broken faith with their Redeemer. Micah 6:8 is not a way to earn salvation by good behavior; it is the shape of life God has always wanted from people He has already redeemed by grace. Verses 6 and 7 are not endorsing child sacrifice but exposing the absurd lengths of a heart that will offer anything except itself. The closing verses describe judgment, not a rule to follow.
basic.originalLanguages: God brings a riv, a lawsuit, against His people. What He requires is summed up in three phrases: mishpat, justice, doing what is right by others; chesed, loyal, merciful covenant love, the very kindness of God; and to walk humbly, from a root meaning lowly and modest, in daily dependence on God. The same chesed that God asks for here returns at the end of the book as the chesed God Himself shows in pardoning sin.
basic.typology: The covenant lawsuit points ahead to the greater judgment, where humanity has no answer to God's just charge. The impossible offerings of verse 7, even a firstborn for sin, find their true and only sufficient answer in the Father who gives His own firstborn Son. And the humble walk with God is embodied perfectly in Jesus, who alone did justice, loved mercy, and walked in lowly obedience.
basic.christ: No pile of sacrifices could ever cover sin; the question of verse 6 hangs unanswered until Christ. He is the one who fulfilled Micah 6:8 flawlessly, and He is the firstborn given for our transgression that verse 7 gropes toward. At the cross God's just lawsuit against His people is answered, not by our offerings, but by His Son, so that mercy and justice meet.
basic.god: God is the just Judge who has a real case against sin, but He is also the wounded Redeemer who pleads, what have I done to you? He does not delight in ritual for its own sake; He wants justice, mercy, and a humble heart. He is patient, rehearsing His past kindness to move His people to return, yet holy enough to sentence dishonest gain and violence.
basic.crossReferences: Micah 6:8 stands with Hosea 6:6, I desire mercy and not sacrifice, and with Jesus in Matthew 23:23 on justice, mercy, and faithfulness. Psalm 51:16-17 and 1 Samuel 15:22 teach that God wants the heart, not mere offerings. Hebrews 10 shows the sacrifices could never take away sin, which Christ alone does.
basic.oneStory: The God who redeemed His people from Egypt now stands as their judge, showing that no amount of religion can substitute for a life of justice, mercy, and humble trust. But the lawsuit no sinner can answer is answered in Christ, the firstborn given for us, who lived the life God requires and bore the sentence we deserved.

context.literary: The chapter is structured as a legal proceeding. It opens with a summons to plead the case before the mountains, calls creation as jury, and lets God speak for the prosecution and the defense at once. The people's frantic questions in verses 6 and 7 build by escalation, from calves to rams to rivers of oil to their own firstborn, only to be cut off by the calm clarity of verse 8. The final movement shifts to the verdict, with rhetorical questions about false scales and a series of futility curses.
context.historical: Micah prophesied in eighth-century Judah, a time of prosperity for the powerful and exploitation of the poor. The short ephah, dishonest scales, and false weights were the everyday tools of merchants cheating buyers. The statutes of Omri and the house of Ahab recall the notoriously wicked northern dynasty whose idolatry and injustice had become a byword; Judah is charged with walking the same road. The reference to Balak and Balaam recalls how God turned a hired curse into blessing on the way to the land.
context.canonical: Micah 6:8 is one of the most quoted verses in the Old Testament, a compact summary of what the Law and the Prophets require. It resonates with Deuteronomy 10:12-13 and Hosea 6:6, and Jesus echoes it in naming justice, mercy, and faithfulness as the weightier matters of the law. The covenant lawsuit form appears also in Isaiah 1 and Hosea 4, and the futility curses reflect the covenant warnings of Deuteronomy 28.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To confront a redeemed people with their ingratitude and injustice through a covenant lawsuit, to strip away the illusion that lavish sacrifice can substitute for a just and humble life, and to announce God's righteous sentence on dishonest gain and violence, so that the people fear the LORD and return to Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The lawsuit and the verdict describe God's dealing with a specific covenant people, yet verse 8 carries an abiding prescription: God's redeemed are to do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly with Him. The impossible offerings of verse 7 are described to be rejected, not imitated; child sacrifice is condemned throughout Scripture.

debates.question: Does Micah 6:8 teach that we are saved by doing justice, mercy, and humility?

views.label: The shape of covenant life, not a way to earn salvation
views.case: Micah addresses a people already redeemed from Egypt by grace; verse 8 describes the life that flows from redemption, the response God has always wanted, not a ladder by which sinners climb to God.

views.label: A moral summary that exposes our need
views.case: Read alongside the whole book, the verse also functions to expose how far short all people fall, driving them to the mercy and pardon that only God provides, fulfilled in Christ at 7:18-20.

debates.bearing: Both readings guard against turning verse 8 into works-righteousness: it is the good life God requires, which no one perfectly renders, and which sends us to the Redeemer who both fulfills it and pardons our failure.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is framed as a riv, a covenant lawsuit, with the mountains summoned as enduring witnesses. God's wounded question, what have I done to you, how have I wearied you, turns the courtroom on its head: the Judge pleads His own innocence and His people's ingratitude. The reply in verses 6 and 7 escalates through the vocabulary of sacrifice, olah, burnt offering, thousands of rams, ten thousand nachal of oil, until it reaches the unthinkable offer of the firstborn, exposing a heart willing to give everything but itself. Then verse 8 answers with three timeless words. Asot mishpat, to do justice, is active right dealing that gives each their due. Ahavat chesed, to love mercy, uses the great covenant word chesed, God's own loyal, merciful love, which His people are not merely to practice but to love. And hatsnea lekhet, to walk humbly, from a root meaning lowly and modest, describes a whole life walked in dependent step with God. The verdict then names the tools of injustice, the short ephah and dishonest scales, and the pattern of Omri and Ahab, before sounding the futility curses: you will eat but not be satisfied, sow but not reap.

typology.type: The covenant lawsuit no sinner can answer
typology.fulfillment: The final judgment where every mouth is stopped, answered for believers by Christ who bore the verdict, Romans 3:19-26.

typology.type: The firstborn offered for transgression, groped after in 6:7
typology.fulfillment: God's own firstborn Son given for our sin, the one sufficient sacrifice, John 3:16 and Hebrews 10:10-14.

typology.type: The life of justice, mercy, and humble walk in 6:8
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, who alone perfectly did justice, loved mercy, and walked humbly with the Father, Philippians 2:5-8.

study.christ: The desperate questions of verses 6 and 7 reach their dead end in verse 7's most extreme offer, the firstborn, and there the gospel waits. What no worshiper could ever bring, God Himself provides: not our firstborn for our transgression, but His firstborn for ours. Jesus is the only one who ever truly did justice, loved mercy, and walked humbly with God, fulfilling verse 8 from the inside. And He is the answer to the lawsuit of verses 1 to 5, for at the cross God's righteous case against His people is fully met, His justice satisfied and His mercy poured out, so that the Judge becomes the Justifier of all who trust His Son. Micah 6:8 read through Christ is not a burden that crushes but a life that grace makes possible, worked in us by the Spirit of the One who lived it first.
study.god: God appears here as the covenant Redeemer turned plaintiff, yet even His prosecution is tender: what have I done to you, how have I wearied you. He rehearses His grace, the exodus, Moses, Aaron, and Miriam, the thwarting of Balaam's curse, to melt hard hearts rather than merely to accuse. He is the God who cannot be bribed with rivers of oil, who sees past the altar to the heart and asks for justice, mercy, and humble dependence. And He is the holy Judge who will not wink at dishonest scales and violent gain, sentencing them to the emptiness of harvests that never satisfy, so that His people might fear His name and turn back.

crossReferences.note: I desire mercy and not sacrifice, closely paralleling what the LORD requires in Micah 6:8.
Jesus names justice, mercy, and faithfulness as the weightier matters of the law, echoing Micah 6:8.
To obey is better than sacrifice, exposing the emptiness of ritual without a surrendered heart.
What does the LORD require of you but to fear Him, walk in His ways, and love Him, the covenant background to Micah 6:8.

study.oneStory: Micah 6 sets the whole human story in a courtroom. The God who redeems has a righteous case against a people who trade justice for gain and try to buy Him off with sacrifice. His requirement is clear and unchanging, justice, mercy, and a humble walk, yet no sinner meets it. So the chapter, like the Bible, leans forward to the One who does: Christ, who lived the life God requires and answered the lawsuit we could not, giving the firstborn we could never give and turning the verdict into pardon for all who come to Him.

sermon.title: What God Requires
sermon.why: Begg preaches Micah 6:1-8, tracing God's covenant lawsuit and the futility of trying to satisfy Him with sacrifice, then unfolding what the LORD truly requires, to do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly with God, and showing that this life is grounded in and made possible by grace rather than a means of earning it.

quiz.q: What kind of scene does Micah 6 open with?

options.text: A covenant lawsuit, with God calling the mountains as witnesses and bringing a case against His people.
options.note: The prophets picture the broken covenant as a court case with creation as witness.

options.text: A coronation of the new king in Jerusalem.
options.note: There is a King in Micah, but chapter 6 is a courtroom, not a coronation.

options.text: A description of a festival with joyful sacrifices.
options.note: Sacrifices are mentioned, but to be relativized, not celebrated; the setting is a lawsuit.

options.text: A private vision with no reference to God's people.
options.note: God explicitly brings a case against His people, Israel.

quiz.q: How does God begin His case against His people in verses 3 to 5?

options.text: By asking what He has done wrong and recalling how He redeemed them from Egypt.
options.note: The wounded question, what have I done to you, rehearses His saving grace to move them to return.

options.text: By immediately listing every sin they have committed.
options.note: The charges come later; He opens with His own kindness and their ingratitude.

options.text: By threatening to destroy the mountains and hills.
options.note: The mountains are witnesses, not targets of the threat.

options.text: By denying that He had ever helped them at all.
options.note: He points precisely to His help: the exodus, Moses, Aaron, Miriam.

quiz.q: What does the LORD require, according to Micah 6:8?

options.text: To act justly, to love mercy, and to walk humbly with your God.
options.note: This is the compact summary of the life God has always wanted from His redeemed people.

options.text: To bring ever larger and more costly sacrifices.
options.note: That is exactly what verses 6 and 7 pile up and verse 8 sets aside.

options.text: To fear the mountains and the foundations of the earth.
options.note: Creation is only the witness; the call is to walk humbly with God.

options.text: Nothing at all, since God asks for no response.
options.note: God clearly shows what is good and requires it of His people.

quiz.q: Why does God reject the offerings imagined in verses 6 and 7?

options.text: Because He wants a just, merciful, and humble heart, not ritual offered in place of it.
options.note: Even the extreme offer of the firstborn cannot substitute for a surrendered life.

options.text: Because burnt offerings were never part of Israel's worship.
options.note: Sacrifice was commanded; the problem is using it to avoid giving the heart.

options.text: Because the calves and rams were not valuable enough.
options.note: The issue is not the size of the gift but the state of the heart.

options.text: Because God is pleased by child sacrifice instead.
options.note: Child sacrifice is condemned; verse 7 exposes a heart that will give anything but itself.

quiz.q: How is this chapter answered in Christ?

options.text: He alone lived the justice, mercy, and humble walk God requires, and He is the firstborn given for our sin.
options.note: Christ fulfills 6:8 and answers the lawsuit no sinner could, giving the firstborn we could never give.

options.text: He taught that God no longer cares about justice or mercy.
options.note: Jesus intensifies these as the weightier matters of the law, Matthew 23:23.

options.text: He offered thousands of rams to satisfy God's case.
options.note: Christ offered Himself once for all, not the sacrifices verse 7 imagines.

options.text: He has no connection to the demands of this chapter.
options.note: Christ is both the fulfiller of 6:8 and the answer to God's lawsuit.

questions: God asks His people, what have I done to you? When you drift from God, does it help to remember His past kindness to you rather than only your failures?
The worshiper in verses 6 and 7 will offer anything except a changed heart. Where are you tempted to give God activity, money, or religion instead of yourself?
Micah 6:8 calls you to do justice. Where in your daily dealings, work, or money is God asking you to treat others rightly and refuse dishonest gain?
To love mercy is to delight in showing loyal kindness because you have received it. Who is God calling you to show mercy to this week?
To walk humbly with God is a whole life of dependence. What would it look like to trade self-reliance for a humble, daily walk with Him?

translationNotes.note: Shittim to Gilgal marks the last wilderness camp and the first camp in the promised land; the journey recalls God bringing Israel safely into Canaan. Shittim can also be rendered Acacia Grove.

To act justly, love mercy, and walk humbly renders three Hebrew phrases: do mishpat (justice), love chesed (steadfast, covenant mercy), and walk humbly (hatsnea lekhet), a whole life lived modestly and dependently with God.

The BSB follows the Septuagint reading you will bear the scorn of the nations; the Hebrew reads the scorn of My people. The statutes of Omri and the house of Ahab recall the wicked northern dynasty as the pattern Judah is condemned for following.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Micah 6:1-16. All 16 verses reproduced contiguously; poetic lines were joined into running prose per the Makor spine style. Chapter 6 contains no em or en dashes in the BSB text. Sermon by Alistair Begg on Micah 6:1-8, What God Requires, verified this session on the Truth For Life Micah scripture index.
